title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management
content: Dual Orexin Receptor Antagonists Suvorexant, lemborexant, and daridorexant block the binding of wake-promoting orexin A and orexin B to OX1 and OX2 receptors, counteracting orexin/hypocretin-mediated nighttime awakening. The FDA does not recommend higher doses of these medications due to safety concerns about the increased risk of next-day driving difficulty, increased daytime somnolence, and narcolepsy-like symptoms, such as hypnogogic-hypnopompic hallucinations, cataplexy, and vivid dreams. These medications are contraindicated in patients with narcolepsy because of possible underlying mechanisms of orexin antagonism.

title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Medication classes:
content: Gamma-aminobutyric acid-A receptor agonists:  Benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists act on GABA subtype A receptors (GABA A Rs) with sedative, anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, and hypnotic effects. A significant difference between the 2 groups is the receptor affinity towards different subtypes of GABA A Rs. Although all benzodiazepines have a similar affinity for various subtypes of alpha subunits, benzodiazepine receptor agonists have varying affinities for different subtypes of alpha subunits. For example, zolpidem and zaleplon have a higher affinity for the alpha-1 subunit and a lower affinity for the alpha-2 and alpha-3 subunits; however, eszopiclone has a higher affinity for the alpha-2 and alpha-3 subunits of the GABA receptor. The potential advantages of benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists are efficacy for sleep onset and maintenance and a range of half-lives. Potential disadvantages associated with benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists include cognitive and motor impairment, next-day sedation, anterograde amnesia, rebound insomnia, nausea, headaches, complex sleep-related behavior, and long-term risks such as dependence, depression, falls, hip fractures, and dementia. The Beers Criteria recommend avoiding benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists in individuals older than 65. [1] [21] [22] The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved 4 benzodiazepines for the treatment of insomnia—temazepam, triazolam, flurazepam, and estazolam. The benzodiazepine receptor agonists approved by the FDA for the treatment of insomnia include eszopiclone, zaleplon, and zolpidem, including extended release and sublingual forms.

title: Chronic Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Other Considerations
content: Benzodiazepines and benzodiazepine receptor agonists can be used for the short-term treatment of insomnia (less than 4 weeks); the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Sedating antidepressants at low doses can be used for short-term treatment of insomnia after consideration of contraindications; the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Orexin receptor antagonists can be used for the treatment of insomnia for up to 3 months; the advantages and disadvantages of longer-term treatment should be discussed with the patient. Antihistamines, antipsychotics, fast-release melatonin, and herbal remedies lack sufficient evidence to support their use in chronic insomnia and may have potential disadvantages. In patients older than 55, extended-release melatonin may be used for up to 3 months for the treatment of insomnia.

title: Chronic Insomnia -- Pearls and Other Issues
content: Key considerations in the management of chronic insomnia include: Chronic insomnia is a common but complex, underdiagnosed disorder that may be related to other medical, psychiatric, and neurological disorders, as well as substance use. Distress, poor work performance, and disability are associated with chronic insomnia. The first-line treatment for chronic insomnia is CBT-I, including online programs. Chronic insomnia may also be treated with medications such as benzodiazepine receptor agonists, dual orexin receptor antagonists, and doxepin. There is limited evidence supporting the use of over-the-counter medications, antipsychotic medications, and other medications to treat chronic insomnia.

title: Short-Term Insomnia -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Therapies
content: The 2017 American Academy of Sleep Medicine Clinical Practice Guideline for the Pharmacologic Treatment of Chronic Insomnia in Adults [20] highlights that pharmacological recommendations are generally weak, with moderate-to-low quality evidence. However, the guideline recommends the treatments listed below. Treatment for sleep onset insomnia: Eszopiclone at strengths of 2 mg or 3 mg at bedtime; ramelteon 8 mg at bedtime; temazepam 15 mg at bedtime; triazolam 0.25 mg at bedtime; zaleplon 5 mg or 10 mg at bedtime; or zolpidem 10 mg at bedtime. Treatment for sleep maintenance insomnia: Doxepin at strengths 3 mg or 6 mg at bedtime; eszopiclone 2 mg or 3 mg at bedtime; temazepam 15 mg at bedtime; suvorexant 10 mg or 15/20 mg or 20 mg at bedtime; or zolpidem 10 mg at bedtime.

title: EMS Flight Safety Decisions -- Issues of Concern -- Fatigue
content: Caffeine is the most commonly used psychoactive drug to promote wakefulness and has been shown to enhance cognitive performance in long-haul aviation conditions. [21] The FAA does not approve the use of prescribed stimulants, or go pills , such as modafinil and dextroamphetamine, for commercial pilots. However, in the military, these medications are used under strict regulations to maintain wakefulness during unavoidable long sorties. Pilots must undergo prior ground testing to assess potential adverse effects before in-flight use. Adverse effects of stimulants include insomnia (the intended adverse effect), decreased appetite, weight loss, hypertension, tachycardia, lightheartedness, headaches, irritability, overconfidence, aggression, and gastrointestinal effects such as diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain. There is also limited evidence of increased tics, so caution is advisable for patients with tic disorders such as Tourette syndrome. Technically, modafinil is not classified as a stimulant, but it does have stimulant-like effects. Like stimulants, modafinil does improve alertness and wakefulness. Modafinil has a slower onset with a half-life of 15 hours, so it tends to have fewer adverse effects than dextroamphetamine. [22] Dextroamphetamine has a shorter half-life of 12 hours with a quicker onset, so it tends to have more noticeable adverse effects and requires more frequent re-dosing. Non-pharmacological management of fatigue is considered the first-line approach. [23]

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
content: Imidazole is a heterocyclic chemical scaffold that serves as a parent structure for several therapeutic agents, most notably the antifungal medication clotrimazole, which is an imidazole derivative. Although ondansetron is sometimes mentioned in connection with the imidazole scaffold, it does not belong to the imidazole class; ondansetron is a serotonin 5‑HT₃‑receptor antagonist primarily used as an antiemetic. Antipsychotics constitute a distinct pharmacological class employed to manage psychotic disorders, and they are not a subtype of ondansetron. A well‑recognized adverse effect of long‑term antipsychotic use is “خلل الحركة المتأخر,” the Arabic term for tardive dyskinesia, a late‑onset movement disorder. These relationships underscore the need to differentiate chemical parent structures from therapeutic categories and to remain vigilant for movement‑related side effects in patients receiving antipsychotic therapy.

All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
content: Anxiolytics constitute a pharmacologic class employed to alleviate anxiety and related disorders. This class encompasses a variety of agents, including the benzodiazepine derivatives bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam, all of which share the characteristic mechanism of enhancing γ‑aminobutyric acid (G‑A) activity at the central nervous system. In addition to benzodiazepines, the anxiolytic category also includes non‑benzodiazepine compounds such as buspiron (buspirone) and proglumid, which act through distinct receptor pathways to produce anxiolytic effects. Recognizing that these diverse medications are all classified as anxiolytics aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, understanding potential drug‑class side effects, and managing patient care across the spectrum of anxiety‑related conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 299
content: Anxiolytics form a therapeutic class used to relieve anxiety and comprise a heterogeneous group of agents including PD 134308, SB 206553, binospirone mesylate, bromazepam, buspirone, chlormezanon, demethyldiazepam, deramciclane, diazepam, estazolam, fluvoxamine, midazolam, prazepam, ritanserin and triazolam. Within this class, diazepam, bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are benzodiazepines that also function as centrally acting muscle relaxants; diazepam and chlormezanon are explicitly classified as centrally acting muscle relaxants in addition to their anxiolytic activity. SB 206553, binospirone mesylate, deramciclane and ritanserin belong to the serotonin‑receptor antagonist subclass, illustrating non‑benzodiazepine mechanisms that block serotonergic receptors, whereas fluvoxamine acts as a selective serotonin‑reuptake inhibitor, linking it to serotonergic modulation but via reuptake inhibition rather than direct receptor antagonism. Important drug‑interaction considerations include contraindications for co‑administration of midazolam with the antiretroviral protease inhibitor saquinavir, and similarly for triazolam with saquinavir, reflecting clinically significant pharmacokinetic interactions likely mediated by cytochrome P450 pathways. Recognizing the shared anxiolytic classification, the distinct pharmacologic actions (benzodiazepine‑mediated GABAergic effects, serotonin receptor antagonism, and serotonin reuptake inhibition), and the specific contraindicated combinations is essential for safe and effective patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1319
content: Anxiolytics comprise a diverse group of medications used to alleviate anxiety, and several specific agents are classified within this category. Bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, and estazolam are all identified as anxiolytic drugs. In addition, medazepam, midazolam, prazepam, and triazolam are likewise recognized as members of the anxiolytic class. Together, these agents represent a range of pharmacologic options—primarily benzodiazepine derivatives and a non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic (buspiron)—that clinicians may select to manage anxiety disorders and related conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1171
content: The community classifies 7‑nitroindazole and SB 206553 as anxiolytic agents, joining a broad anxiolytic group that comprises benzodiazepine derivatives (binospirone mesylate, bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam, triazolam), non‑benzodiazepine agents (buspiron, chlormezanon, deramciclane, fluvoxamine, flurazepam) and the serotonin‑2 receptor antagonist ritanserin, which is also listed as an anxiolytic. Many of these anxiolytics act through modulation of the GABAergic system; consequently they are linked to the GABA‑modulating (GABA modulátory) class. Flurazepam is noted for its dual classification as an anxiolytic and as an inverse member of both the GABA‑modulating and anxiolytic (anxiolytika) categories, reflecting its complex interaction with GABA pathways. In terms of safety, midazolam and triazolam are contraindicated when co‑administered with the protease inhibitor saquinavir, highlighting a risk of adverse drug‑drug interactions. SB 206553, as well as pindolol, binospirone mesylate, cyanopindolol, deramciclane, and ritanserin, are identified as serotonin antagonists, whereas fluvoxamine, although related to the serotonin system, is not a direct serotonin antagonist. Distinguishing true serotonin antagonists from agents with limited serotonergic activity is emphasized for appropriate selection of treatments targeting serotonin receptors in anxiety, depression, and other neuropsychiatric disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
content: 7‑nitroindazole, bromazepam, buspiron, chlormezanon, demethyldiazepam, estazolam and prazepam are all classified as anxiolytics, a therapeutic group of agents used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. These compounds, despite differing in chemical structure—such as the benzodiazepine derivatives bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam and prazepam, and the non‑benzodiazepine agents 7‑nitroindazole, buspiron and chlormezanon—share the common pharmacological purpose of reducing excessive nervous system arousal. Recognizing that each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic class helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options for anxiety disorders and understand their related mechanisms of action and potential side‑effect profiles.
